title: Liṅga-Purāṇa 1
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 6th-10th c. CE (composite, with later additions)
summary: The first part (pūrvabhāga) of the Liṅga-Purāṇa, one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas and a major Śaiva text centered on the worship of Śiva in his aniconic form as the liṅga. It treats cosmogony, the manifestation and glory of Śiva, the origin of the liṅga, sacred geography, yogic and devotional practice, vows, and the standard Puranic topics of creation, genealogies, and pilgrimage.
history: Authorship and exact date are unknown; the text is a layered, composite compilation revised over several centuries between roughly the 6th and 10th centuries CE, with possible medieval interpolations. It exists in multiple recensions and is a key source for the theology of liṅga worship and the Pāśupata-influenced Śaiva tradition.

title: Liṅga-Purāṇa 2,1–55
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 6th-10th c. CE (composite, with later additions)
summary: Chapters 1–55 of the second part (uttarabhāga) of the Liṅga-Purāṇa, continuing the Śaiva subject matter of the work. This portion expands on the glorification of Śiva, ritual and devotional worship of the liṅga, narratives of Śiva's deeds, sacred sites, and instructions on vows, gifts, and observances.
history: Part of the same composite Śaiva Mahāpurāṇa as the first part; the uttarabhāga is generally considered a somewhat later stratum within the text's centuries-long redactional history. Transmitted in multiple recensions and printed editions.

title: Mārkaṇḍeya-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 3rd-6th c. CE; Devīmāhātmya layer c. 5th-6th c. CE (composite)
summary: Chapters 1–93 of the Mārkaṇḍeya-Purāṇa, one of the oldest of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, framed as a dialogue involving the sage Mārkaṇḍeya. It covers cosmogony, dharma, accounts of yoga and rebirth, royal genealogies (the Vaivasvata Manu), and famously embeds the Devīmāhātmya (Durgā-saptaśatī), the foundational text of Goddess worship celebrating the Devī's victory over the demons Madhu-Kaiṭabha, Mahiṣāsura, and Śumbha-Niśumbha.
history: Considered one of the earliest Puranas, with an old narrative core perhaps from the early centuries CE; the Devīmāhātmya section (chapters 81–93) is a later but highly influential interpolation (c. 5th-6th c. CE) central to Śākta tradition. The not_after hint of c. 600 CE accords with the dating of this Goddess-hymn layer.

title: Matsya-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 3rd-5th c. CE, with material to c. 6th-10th c. CE (composite)
summary: One of the oldest of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, narrated by Viṣṇu in his Matsya (fish) avatāra to King Manu during the cosmic deluge. It is encyclopedic, covering cosmogony and dissolution, royal and divine genealogies, the churning of the ocean, accounts of avatāras, temple and image construction, vows and gifts (dāna), pilgrimage (especially Prayāga and Vārāṇasī), and statecraft.
history: Regarded as among the earliest Purāṇas with an old core, though expanded over centuries; it preserves relatively archaic Puranic material and important dynastic king-lists used by historians. The work_id 176 likely denotes a particular chapter range or section within the larger text.

title: Nārada-Purāṇa 1 [Nāradīya-Purāṇa 1]
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (pūrvabhāga; composite, with later additions)
summary: The first part (pūrvabhāga) of the Nārada-Purāṇa (also called Nāradīya-Purāṇa), one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, strongly Vaiṣṇava and bhakti-oriented and presented through the teachings of the sage Nārada. It deals with dharma, devotion to Viṣṇu, vows and festivals, the methods of liberation, and notably contains digests on subjects such as grammar, prosody, astronomy/astrology, and a famous summary of the contents of all eighteen Mahāpurāṇas.
history: A relatively late Mahāpurāṇa whose pūrvabhāga is usually dated to the early medieval period (c. 9th-11th c. CE); it is valued for its self-referential descriptions of the Puranic corpus and for its encyclopedic treatment of ancillary sciences. Transmitted in two main bhāgas.

title: Nārada-Purāṇa 2 [Nāradīya-Purāṇa 2]
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
summary: The second part (uttarabhāga) of the Nārada-Purāṇa, continuing the Vaiṣṇava and bhakti themes of the work. It includes the Rukmāṅgada-carita (the story of King Rukmāṅgada and the Ekādaśī vow), extensive treatment of pilgrimage sites (tīrthas), the merits of devotional observances, and instruction on attaining liberation through devotion to Viṣṇu.
history: The uttarabhāga is generally regarded as a later stratum of the Nārada-Purāṇa than the pūrvabhāga and is noted for its strong emphasis on the Ekādaśī vow and tīrtha-māhātmya material; some sections show late medieval expansion.

title: Narasiṃha-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 5th-8th c. CE (disputed; possibly earlier core)
summary: A Vaiṣṇava Upapurāṇa centered on the worship of Viṣṇu, especially in his man-lion (Narasiṃha) avatāra who slays the demon Hiraṇyakaśipu to protect the devotee Prahlāda. It treats cosmogony, the avatāras of Viṣṇu, dharma and vows, genealogies of solar and lunar dynasties, and devotional and eschatological teaching.
history: Classed among the Upapurāṇas; it is relatively early among them, with some scholars (e.g., R. C. Hazra) dating its core to roughly the 5th c. CE while others place it later. It is an important source for early Vaiṣṇava theology surrounding the Narasiṃha avatāra.

title: Śiva-Purāṇa 7 [Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā]
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
summary: The Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā, the seventh saṃhitā (section) of the Śiva-Purāṇa, one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas devoted to Śiva. Narrated in the voice of Vāyu, it expounds Śaiva cosmology, the supremacy of Śiva, the nature of the liṅga, devotional and yogic practice, the Pāśupata teachings, ritual worship, and the path to liberation through Śiva.
history: The Śiva-Purāṇa as transmitted is a composite of multiple saṃhitās compiled at different periods; the Vāyavīya-Saṃhitā (in two parts, pūrva and uttara) overlaps substantially with material in the Vāyu-Purāṇa and reflects the medieval consolidation of the Śaiva Puranic corpus. Several recensions of the Śiva-Purāṇa circulate.

title: Skanda-Purāṇa: Revākhaṇḍa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 12th-15th c. CE (composite; manuscripts as late as 17th c.)
summary: The Revākhaṇḍa, a section devoted to the glorification (māhātmya) of the Narmadā River (Revā) and its many tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) along its course, here transmitted within the printed Skanda-Purāṇa. It narrates the sanctity, origin myths, and pilgrimage rewards of bathing and worship at the sacred fords of the Narmadā.
history: Scholars note that the lengthy Revā/Reva Khaṇḍa (c. 232 chapters) printed in the Skanda-Purāṇa by the Veṅkaṭeśvara Press (1910) is in fact the Revākhaṇḍa originally belonging to the Vāyu-Purāṇa, mistakenly incorporated into the Skanda; the genuine Skanda recension is shorter (c. 116 chapters). The only surviving manuscript of one Revākhaṇḍa recension dates to 1682.

title: Vāmana-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
summary: One of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, nominally centered on Viṣṇu's dwarf (Vāmana) avatāra who subdues the demon-king Bali, though much of its content is in fact strongly Śaiva and Śākta. It covers cosmogony and sacred geography, myths of Śiva and the Goddess, pilgrimage (especially Kurukṣetra and Prayāga), vows, and the standard Puranic topics.
history: Dated to roughly the early medieval period; the surviving text differs considerably from older descriptions of a 'Vāmana-Purāṇa' and circulates in more than one recension (notably a 'Saromāhātmya' recension). It blends Vaiṣṇava, Śaiva, and Śākta material, reflecting its layered composition.

title: Vāmana-Purāṇa [Saromāhātmya recension]
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
summary: A second transmission of the Vāmana-Purāṇa, distinguished by its 'sm' suffix which most plausibly designates the Saromāhātmya recension/appendix — a glorification of sacred lakes (saras) and tīrthas associated with the Vāmana-Purāṇa. Like the main text it combines the Vāmana avatāra narrative with substantial Śaiva and tīrtha-māhātmya material.
history: The Vāmana-Purāṇa survives in distinct recensions; this entry, sharing the same title as the preceding record, evidently captures a separate (likely Saromāhātmya) recension or appended section. Identification of the exact recension is somewhat uncertain.

title: Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa: Atharvavidhikathana [Atharvavidhāna]
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A section of the Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa (an Upapurāṇa appended to the Viṣṇu-Purāṇa) treating the 'Atharvavidhāna' — the prescription of Atharvavedic rites, mantras, and ritual procedures, here spanning chapters around 2.127 (khaṇḍa 2). It presents the application of Atharvaveda-based ritual, expiation, and apotropaic practice within the Purana's encyclopedic framework.
history: The Viṣṇudharmottara is a vast, encyclopedic Upapurāṇa in three khaṇḍas, best known for the Citrasūtra section on painting; its core is generally dated to roughly the 5th-7th c. CE. This Atharvavidhāna portion reflects the text's incorporation of Vedic-ritual and śāstric subject matter.

title: Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A portion of the third khaṇḍa of the Viṣṇudharmottara-Purāṇa (around chapters 33–43), which most famously contains the Citrasūtra — a foundational treatise on the theory and practice of painting (citra), as well as related material on iconography, image-making, dance, music, and the arts. The work is an encyclopedic Upapurāṇa attached to the Viṣṇu-Purāṇa.
history: The Viṣṇudharmottara is celebrated by art historians for the Citrasūtra (3.35–43), the earliest surviving Indian text on painting, and for its iconographic prescriptions; the third khaṇḍa is a principal source for classical Indian art theory. The core is usually dated to about the 5th-7th c. CE.

title: Viṣṇu-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Parāśara)
date_estimate: c. 4th-5th c. CE (core; disputed, possibly earlier)
summary: One of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas and a model 'pañcalakṣaṇa' Purana, framed as the teaching of the sage Parāśara to Maitreya. It is the central Vaiṣṇava Purana, treating cosmogony and cosmic dissolution, genealogies of gods and kings, the deeds and avatāras of Viṣṇu (including the life of Kṛṣṇa), geography, dharma, and the four yugas, with a strongly devotional and Vaiṣṇava-theistic orientation.
history: Often regarded as one of the oldest and most systematically structured Mahāpurāṇas, with its core frequently dated to the Gupta period (c. 4th-5th c. CE), though parts may be earlier or later. It received the influential commentary of Śrīdhara Svāmin and is foundational for the Vaiṣṇava tradition.

title: Revākhaṇḍa of the Vāyupurāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Vyāsa)
summary: The Revākhaṇḍa belonging to the Vāyu-Purāṇa, a large (c. 232-chapter) glorification of the Narmadā (Revā) River and its pilgrimage sites, narrating the river's sanctity, origin myths, and the merits of bathing and worship at its many tīrthas. This is the original Vāyu-Purāṇa Revākhaṇḍa, distinct from (though often conflated with) the shorter one in the Skanda-Purāṇa.
history: Modern scholarship holds that the long Revākhaṇḍa printed within the Skanda-Purāṇa (Veṅkaṭeśvara Press, 1910) actually originates in the Vāyu-Purāṇa and was wrongly attached to the Skanda; this record reflects that genuine Vāyu-Purāṇa attribution. It is an important Narmadā tīrtha-māhātmya.

title: Śāstravārtāsamuccaya
author: Haribhadrasūri
summary: A Jain philosophical work of some 700 verses surveying, examining, and critically engaging the doctrines of the major non-Jain schools and sects — including Buddhist, Sāṃkhya, Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, Mīmāṃsā, materialist, and theistic positions — refuting and reconciling them while upholding the Jain (anekānta) standpoint, treated with notable respect toward opponents. Haribhadra furnished it with his own auto-commentary, the Dikpradā (c. 2250 verses).
history: Traditionally Haribhadra was placed c. 459-529 CE, but on the basis of his familiarity with Dharmakīrti, modern scholars (following Jinavijaya, 1919) date him after c. 650, and the Śāstravārtāsamuccaya is commonly regarded as an 8th-century work — consistent with the project's not_before/not_after hints of 700-799. It is a key Jain doxographical and inclusivist text.

title: Satyaśāsanaparīkṣā
author: Vidyānanda (Vidyānandin)
summary: An 'Investigation into the True Teaching,' a Digambara Jain philosophical work that presents and refutes twelve rival Indian philosophical systems — including Puruṣādvaita (Advaita Vedānta), Śabdādvaita (grammarians), Vijñānādvaita (Yogācāra), Citrādvaita, Cārvāka, Sautrāntika Buddhism, theistic and non-theistic Sāṃkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Nyāya, and Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara Mīmāṃsā — in order to establish Jainism as the sole 'true teaching.'
history: Composed by the Digambara logician Vidyānandin (Vidyānanda), generally placed in the 9th-10th c. CE. Known since around 1920 but long understudied, it was first edited by Gokul Chandra Jain (1964); Jens W. Borgland produced a critical edition and study (Harrassowitz, 2020). It is an important source for late first-millennium inter-school polemic.

title: Stavacintāmaṇi
author: Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa
date_estimate: c. 9th c. CE (between Vasugupta and Somānanda)
summary: The 'Wish-fulfilling Jewel of Praise,' a hymn of about 120 verses addressed to Śiva (Paramaśiva) as the Supreme Reality. Highly devotional yet philosophically profound, its sustained theme is that love and devotion to Śiva are everything, expressing the non-dual (advaita) recognition of individual consciousness as one with universal Śiva-consciousness.
history: Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa is placed in the formative period of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism, between Vasugupta and Somānanda (roughly 9th c. CE). The text was commented upon by Kṣemarāja, and the Sanskrit was first published with his commentary in the Kashmir Series of Texts and Studies (vol. X, 1918).

title: Bījanighaṇṭu
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to Bhairava)
summary: A tantric lexicon (nighaṇṭu) that decodes and lists the bīja (seed) syllables used in mantras, giving the conventional code-names by which the individual phonemes/seeds are designated in tantric ritual literature. It functions as a reference key enabling practitioners to reconstruct mantras written in coded form.
history: Traditionally framed as spoken by Bhairava, it is one of the dictionary-texts collected in the Tantrābhidhāna ('Dictionaries of Tantra Śāstra') edited in the Tantric Texts Series by Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe), alongside the Mātṛkānighaṇṭu, Mudrānighaṇṭu, and similar works. Its precise date and authorship are unknown.

title: Cakrapāṇinātha: Bhāvopahārastotra
author: Cakrapāṇinātha
summary: The Bhāvopahāra ('The Offering of Inner States/Flowers to Śiva'), a Trika hymn to Śiva in which all of one's mental states, sense-activities, and experiences are offered up as worship, expressing the non-dual recognition that every act of consciousness is itself an oblation to Śiva. It is a concise but philosophically dense devotional work of Kashmir Śaivism.
history: The author names himself only obliquely as 'someone'; the commentator Ramyadeva Bhaṭṭa, in the Bhāvopahāravivaraṇa, identifies him through wordplay as Cakrapāṇinātha (or the Lord who holds the wheel). The hymn is generally dated to the 11th century CE and belongs to the non-dual Trika stream of Kashmir Śaivism.
